chunk_summary: This section provides worked examples for managing fluid-restricted and enteral-failure states in pediatric patients, focusing on numeric enteral prescriptions and advancement targets from day 0–3.
learning_objectives: Understand numeric enteral prescription strategies for fluid-restricted pediatric patients.
Learn how to optimize calorie and protein delivery in enteral nutrition using concentrated formulas.
Recognize when to initiate parenteral nutrition in cases of enteral failure.
Apply guidelines for tapering parenteral nutrition as enteral intake improves.
key_facts: Fluid-restricted CHD example: A 4-month-old infant weighing 5.0 kg is capped at 100 mL/kg/day total fluids (500 mL/day).
Baseline needs for 0–6 months: 90 kcal/kg/day and 1.24 g/kg/day protein (450 kcal/day and 6.2 g/day protein).
Switching to 1.5 kcal/mL formula allows 600 kcal/day in 400 mL/day, preserving 100 mL/day for non-feed fluids.
Oncology mucositis example: A 12-year-old (30 kg) requires minimum 1.5 g/kg/day protein (45 g/day) during parenteral nutrition.
Temporary PN should start at or below estimated REE (Schofield without stress factors) and advance as recovery progresses.
Daily totals from PN, EN, and other fluids must be documented to avoid overfeeding and electrolyte imbalances.
reference_quote: Baseline needs for 0–6 months are about 90 kcal/kg/day and 1.24 g/kg/day protein, as per Nutrient requirements for Indians 2020 (ICMR–NIN).
document_key_topics: Parenteral nutrition initiation in enteral failure
Calorie and protein optimization in pediatric patients
Advancement targets for day 0–3 in critical care nutrition
